ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Sa Kei Ma (Saqima). Mapped to the nearest parent class 'snack food product' (FOODON_03301521) as a defensible approximate anchor. A more specific term does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: Sa Kei Ma is a deep-fried egg-dough cake bound with syrup. Mapped to FoodEx2 'Pastry and cakes' (A0CKL) as the closest defensible category. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this traditional Chinese confection.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for Sa Kei Ma. SNOMED CT coverage of traditional Chinese confections is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Sa Kei Ma is approximately 40 g (one to two small pieces), as commonly sold in Singapore provision shops and supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Sa Kei Ma — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sa Kei Ma (CR ID F11090054). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry for fried Chinese pastry (NDB 18964) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown separately; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Sa Kei Ma (Saqima) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: refined wheat-flour pastry / sweet biscuit with sugar syrup coating, consistent with GI values of 60–70 reported for similar deep-fried refined-flour confections in Atkinson et al. (2008) and Brand-Miller et al. peer-reviewed tables. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (40 g × 53.6 g/100 g = 21.4 g) / 100 = 13.9, rounded to 14.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (26.6 g/100 g) with negligible fibre (0.5 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; estimated saturated fat (~12 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Sa Kei Ma due to its high sugar content (26.6 g/100 g) promoting insulin-driven sodium retention and its high saturated fat content contributing to vascular stiffness; sodium at 110 mg/100 g is moderate but the overall metabolic burden is high.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 26.6 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling; total refined carbohydrate 53.6 g/100 g with negligible fibre (0.5 g/100 g) causes rapid postprandial glucose spike; estimated GI 65 yields a glycemic load of 14 per 40 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sa Kei Ma should be avoided by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: its sugar content of 26.6 g/100 g exceeds safe thresholds, dietary fibre is negligible at 0.5 g/100 g providing no glucose-buffering effect, and the estimated glycemic load of 14 per serving will cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation with estimated saturated fat ~12 g/100 g and total fat 29 g/100 g; high sugar (26.6 g/100 g) promotes dyslipidaemia and triglyceride elevation; potential trans fat from shortening used in traditional preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sa Kei Ma is high-risk for individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its high total fat (29 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~12 g/100 g), high sugar content (26.6 g/100 g) promoting hypertriglyceridaemia, and deep-fried preparation that may introduce trans fats from partially hydrogenated shortening.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sa Kei Ma (沙琪玛, also romanized as Saqima or Sachima) is a traditional Chinese confection widely consumed in Singapore and across Chinese communities in Southeast Asia. It is made from wheat flour and egg, which are combined into a soft dough, cut into thin strips, deep-fried until golden and crisp, then bound together with a hot sugar-malt syrup and pressed into a dense, chewy cake block. The texture is simultaneously crispy and chewy, with a sweet, caramel-like flavour from the malt syrup. Sa Kei Ma is classified as a local ethnic snack in Singapore and is commonly sold in provision shops, Chinese bakeries, and supermarkets. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 493 kcal per 100 g, with high total fat (29 g/100 g), high sugar (26.6 g/100 g), and moderate protein (4.2 g/100 g). Dietary fibre is negligible at 0.5 g/100 g. The deep-fried preparation and refined-flour base give it a high estimated glycemic index (~65, medium category), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The high saturated fat content (~12 g/100 g estimated) and potential trans fat from traditional shortening further elevate cardiovascular risk. Despite its vegetarian status, it is not suitable for vegan diets due to egg content. A typical serving is approximately 40 g (one to two pieces), delivering approximately 197 kcal and a glycemic load of approximately 14.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat Sa Kei Ma?
How many calories in Sa Kei Ma?
What is 沙琪玛 made of?
Is Sa Kei Ma suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of Saqima?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo (CR ID F11090054) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry and documented. GI estimated from peer-reviewed analogous food data (Atkinson et al. 2008) and documented. All mandatory multilingual names provided. Ontology mapped to defensible approximate anchors with gap notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (12.0 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry for deep-fried Chinese pastry (NDB 18964), as the HPB Foodo database does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. This value should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Sa Kei Ma are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat-flour deep-fried confection data from Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008), International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. No direct GI measurement for Sa Kei Ma was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; traditional recipes use egg and vegetable oil but commercial variants may vary — consumers should verify packaging. 5. Trans fat presence is plausible in traditional preparations using partially hydrogenated shortening; commercial variants may differ.
